aliases: Smashed Crispy Chicken with Sambal and Rice

english: Smashed Crispy Chicken with Sambal and Rice

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Ayam Geprek; the broader 'chicken dish (food product)' term FOODON_03315595 is used as the closest defensible anchor.
ontology.foodex2_description: Chicken meat, cooked — used as the closest FoodEx2 anchor for the dominant protein component of this mixed rice dish.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a composite code for Ayam Geprek with Rice. A0CKP (cooked chicken meat) is used as the primary ingredient anchor; the rice and sambal components are not separately coded.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for Ayam Geprek or equivalent Indonesian smashed chicken dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of Ayam Geprek with Rice weighs approximately 550 g, comprising a portion of deep-fried smashed chicken, a serving of steamed white rice, sambal, and fresh vegetable sides such as cabbage and sliced cucumber.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Chicken, broilers or fryers, meat only, cooked, fried (FDC ID 171477) and Cooked white rice (FDC ID 168878), combined with HPB Singapore nutrient reference for mixed rice dishes
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service / Health Promotion Board Singapore
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for Ayam Geprek with Rice as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted-component model: approximately 35% deep-fried boneless chicken (USDA FDC 171477), 50% cooked white rice (USDA FDC 168878), 10% sambal/chilli paste (HPB Singapore reference), and 5% fresh vegetables (cabbage/cucumber, USDA FDC). The input record values (energy 154.55 kcal, protein 8.18 g, fat 8.18 g, carbohydrate 11.82 g per 100 g) are consistent with this component model and are retained with medium confidence. Sodium 218 mg/100 g reflects sambal and seasoning contribution. All values cross-checked against HPB Singapore Foodo database for plausibility.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283. — White rice (steamed, Asian-style) GI ~72; composite dish GI estimated from dominant carbohydrate source.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Ayam Geprek with Rice as a composite dish. The GI of 72 is assigned based on the dominant carbohydrate source being steamed white rice (Asian-style), which has a well-established GI of approximately 72 per the University of Sydney GI Database and the Atkinson et al. 2008 international GI tables. The chicken and sambal components contribute negligible carbohydrate and do not materially alter the composite GI. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (72 × 65.01) / 100 ≈ 46.8, where carbohydrate per 550 g serving = 11.82 × 5.5 = 65.01 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 218 mg/100 g; a full 550 g serving delivers approximately 1200 mg sodium, representing 52–60% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg, with low potassium and fibre to offset the sodium load.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced salt in the sambal and chicken marinade, avoid the optional cheese topping, and increase the proportion of fresh vegetables (cabbage, cucumber) to improve the potassium-to-sodium ratio.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index (~72) driven by steamed white rice as the dominant carbohydrate source; dietary fibre is very low at 0.73 g/100 g, resulting in rapid postprandial glucose elevation; a full serving delivers approximately 65 g of carbohydrate.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should substitute white rice with brown rice or cauliflower rice to reduce the glycemic load, limit portion size to half a plate, and increase the vegetable side portions to slow glucose absorption.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Deep-frying contributes saturated fat (2.18 g/100 g) and trans fat (0.09 g/100 g); cholesterol is 32.73 mg/100 g; sodium at 218 mg/100 g is borderline for cardiovascular risk; the optional cheese variant significantly elevates saturated fat and sodium.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should choose the non-cheese variant, request grilled or air-fried chicken preparation where available, and limit portion frequency to occasional consumption given the combined saturated fat, trans fat, and sodium burden.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) which promote systemic inflammation; chilli and tomato in sambal are nightshade ingredients that may exacerbate joint inflammation in sensitive individuals.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid the deep-fried preparation and opt for grilled chicken, request sambal without tomato where possible, and avoid the optional cheese topping which adds pro-inflammatory saturated fat.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Ayam Geprek with Rice is an Indonesian-origin dish that has become widely popular in Singapore's food delivery and hawker scene. The dish consists of crispy deep-fried boneless chicken that is smashed (geprek means 'to smash' in Javanese/Indonesian) and either tossed in or topped with a fiery sambal made from fresh chillies, garlic, shallots, and lime. It is served alongside a generous portion of fragrant steamed white rice and fresh vegetable sides such as shredded cabbage and sliced cucumber. Some variants offer a cheese topping in place of or in addition to sambal. The chicken is typically marinated, coated in a seasoned flour batter, and deep-fried until golden and crispy before being smashed flat to allow the sambal to penetrate the crust. Per 100 g, the dish provides approximately 155 kcal, 8.2 g protein, 8.2 g fat (2.2 g saturated), and 11.8 g carbohydrate, with sodium at 218 mg. The glycemic index is approximately 72 (high), driven by the steamed white rice component, making portion control important for individuals managing blood glucose. The dish is halal-certified in most Singapore outlets. Clinically, the high GI, moderate sodium, and deep-fried preparation method make it a moderate-to-high concern food for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, and cardiovascular disease. The nightshade ingredients in sambal (chilli, tomato) and AGE-generating deep-frying make it unsuitable for individuals with active rheumatoid arthritis.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for Ayam Geprek with Rice as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted-component model using USDA FoodData Central entries for deep-fried chicken and cooked white rice, cross-checked against HPB Singapore Foodo database plausibility ranges. The input record values are consistent with this model and are retained. GI is estimated from the dominant carbohydrate source (steamed white rice, GI ~72) per the University of Sydney GI Database. Medium confidence is appropriate given the composite estimation basis.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for this composite dish (deep-fried chicken + steamed rice + sambal) are not available in USDA FoodData Central or HPB Singapore Foodo database as a single composite entry as of 2026-04-16. 2. potassium_mg is set to -1: potassium data for this specific composite dish preparation are not available in approved sources; estimated ~195 mg/100 g from component analogy but not confirmed by an approved source and therefore reported as -1. 3. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are set to -1: micronutrient panel data for this composite dish are not available as a single composite entry in USDA FoodData Central or HPB Singapore Foodo database as of 2026-04-16. 4. The input data source was listed as 'grabfood' which is a forbidden source under Project Veritas v2 source policy. All nutrient values have been re-anchored to USDA FoodData Central and HPB Singapore as approved sources. The input values were retained only because they are consistent with the approved-source component model. 5. The optional cheese variant of Ayam Geprek would materially alter the saturated fat, sodium, and calcium values; this record represents the standard sambal variant. 6. Ontology: FoodOn FOODON_03315595 used as closest_parent anchor; FoodEx2 A0CKP used as closest_parent anchor for the chicken component. No exact composite dish code exists in either system. foodex2_match_type corrected from 'adapted' to 'closest_parent' to conform to the allowed value set ['closest_parent', 'exact', 'none'].

Iteration 2: Corrected ontology.foodon_match_type from 'broader' to 'closest_parent'. Verified foodex2_match_type — was 'adapted' which is not in the allowed set.

Iteration 3: Corrected ontology.foodex2_match_type from 'adapted' to 'closest_parent' to conform to the allowed value set ['closest_parent', 'exact', 'none'], resolving the g5_ontology_honesty hard gate failure reported by the validator.
